front: Who introduced the Relational Model and in which year?
back: Edgar F. Codd introduced the Relational Model in 1970 while working at IBM.

front: What is a Relation in the Relational Model?
back: A Relation is a table with rows (tuples) and columns (attributes) that represents a collection of related data entities.
A Tuple is a single row in a relation, representing a single record with all its attribute values.
An Attribute is a column in a relation that represents a specific property or characteristic of an entity.

front: Define Domain in the context of Relational Model.
back: Domain is the set of atomic (indivisible) values that an attribute can take, defining the type and valid range of data.

front: What is Degree of a relation?
back: Degree is the number of attributes (columns) in a relation. A relation with n attributes has degree n.

front: What is Cardinality of a relation?
back: Cardinality is the number of tuples (rows) in a relation at a given point in time. It changes as tuples are inserted or deleted.
Super Key is a set of one or more attributes that uniquely identifies each tuple in a relation. It can have redundant attributes.
Candidate Key is a minimal super key (no proper subset is a key) that uniquely identifies tuples. There can be multiple candidate keys.
Primary Key is the selected candidate key used to uniquely identify tuples in a relation. It cannot contain NULL values.
Foreign Key is an attribute in a relation that references the Primary Key of another relation, establishing a relationship between tables.
Primary key values must be unique and not NULL. This ensures each entity is uniquely identifiable.
Foreign key values must either match a primary key value in the referenced relation or be NULL.

front: What is NULL value in Relational Model?
back: NULL represents missing or unknown information. It is not the same as zero or empty string and indicates absence of a value.

front: What is the Selection operation in Relational Algebra?
back: Selection (σ) is a unary operation that selects tuples (rows) from a relation based on a specified condition/predicate.
